principles.label: Local before fallback
principles.detail: A supplied local laboratory interval always overrides the built-in age/sex fallback.

principles.label: Rules, not probabilities
principles.detail: The engine evaluates explicit Boolean conditions. Candidate levels and priority points are ordinal workflow labels, not calibrated probabilities.

principles.label: Missingness stays visible
principles.detail: Absent data do not become normal findings; they generate limitations or adaptive questions when a rule requires them.

principles.label: Escalation precedes etiology
principles.detail: Emergency and urgent findings are surfaced before the differential is interpreted.

steps.title: Establish scope and urgency
steps.clinicalQuestion: Can this general-pediatric pathway be applied safely, and does the case require immediate escalation before etiologic classification?
steps.summary: Validate age and reference-range provenance, then screen instability, bleeding, cytopenias, blasts, microangiopathy, and lead thresholds before interpreting the anemia pattern.

inputs.purpose: Determines whether built-in pediatric ranges are available and selects the fallback age band.
Selects the sex-specific fallback interval within the relevant age band.
Override every built-in hemoglobin, MCV, and RDW fallback.
Surfaces hemodynamic, respiratory, neurologic, cardiac, and active-bleeding escalation conditions.
Detects additional cytopenias using supplied local limits or local abnormal flags.
Flags blasts and schistocytes before routine differential ranking.
Applies CDC escalation and venous-confirmation pathways.
Adds limitations because transfusion and altitude can alter interpretation.

equations.label: Built-in scope gate
equations.expression: supportedAge = (6 ≤ ageMonths < 216)
equations.interpretation: Below 6 months or at/above 18 years, the engine will not rely on its general-pediatric fallback ranges.

equations.label: Additional cytopenia count
equations.expression: count = I(leukopenia) + I(neutropenia) + I(thrombocytopenia)
equations.interpretation: One or more affected non-red-cell lineages increases concern for marrow failure, infiltration, malignancy, infection, or systemic disease.

equations.label: CDC lead routing
equations.expression: BLL ≥ 3.5 → lead pathway; capillary BLL ≥ 3.5 → venous confirmation; BLL 20–44 and BLL ≥ 45 → higher urgency tiers
equations.interpretation: Specimen type changes the next action; a capillary screen is not treated as a confirmed venous result.

outputs: Scope status and effective range provenance
Emergency, urgent, important, or informational alerts
Interpretive limitations for age, transfusion, altitude, or missing ranges
Permission to continue into anemia classification without suppressing alerts

examples.title: Marrow red flags override routine pattern review
examples.data: Age 84 months; fever; ANC 0.5; platelets 42; smear reports blasts.
examples.evaluation: Supported age, but fever with neutropenia, anemia with additional cytopenias, and blasts each match escalation rules.
examples.result: Emergency/urgent alerts are displayed before the marrow-failure or malignancy pattern is reviewed.

examples.title: Capillary lead result changes the next step
examples.result: Lead pathway is matched and venous confirmation is explicitly requested.

evidence.use: Supports early review of symptoms, smear, reticulocytes, and multi-lineage abnormalities in pediatric anemia.
Supplies current blood-lead reference and specimen-specific action tiers.
Supplies the built-in CBC fallback table and the severe-IDA hemoglobin category used only as an alerting context.
Supports contextual, population-aware hemoglobin interpretation rather than one universal cutoff.

steps.caveat: Hemoglobin below 7 g/dL is shown as the AAP severe iron-deficiency-anemia category only when IDA is the relevant diagnosis; it is not encoded as a universal transfusion threshold.

steps.title: Confirm anemia
steps.clinicalQuestion: Is the measured hemoglobin below the most appropriate lower reference limit for this patient?
steps.summary: Compare hemoglobin with a supplied local lower limit first; otherwise use the age/sex AAP fallback only within the supported age scope.

inputs.purpose: Primary value being classified.
Preferred decision threshold when supplied.
Selects fallback age band when no local limit is provided.
Selects the appropriate fallback within an age band.
Adds a limitation; this prototype does not calculate altitude adjustment.

equations.label: Effective hemoglobin threshold
equations.expression: HbLower = localHbLower ?? AAPFallback(ageMonths, sexAtBirth)
equations.interpretation: The null-coalescing choice means a local value wins; fallback is used only when the local value is absent.

equations.label: Anemia classification
equations.expression: anemia = (hemoglobin < HbLower)
equations.interpretation: Equality to the lower limit is not classified as below range by the current implementation.

equations.label: Distance below threshold
equations.expression: Hb deficit = HbLower − measured Hb
equations.interpretation: Displayed examples may use this arithmetic to show magnitude, but the deficit itself is not a scoring variable.

outputs: Anemia present, absent, or indeterminate
Measured hemoglobin and effective lower limit
Threshold source: local laboratory or AAP fallback
Age-band label and scope limitation when applicable

examples.title: 20-month-old female
examples.data: Hemoglobin 8.4 g/dL; no local lower limit; AAP fallback 11.0 g/dL.
examples.result: Anemia present. The algorithm continues to morphology and marrow response.

examples.title: Iron deficiency can exist without anemia
examples.data: Hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL; effective lower limit 11.3 g/dL; ferritin 10 ng/mL.
examples.evaluation: 11.6 is not below 11.3, while ferritin is below the applicable AAP threshold.
examples.result: No anemia by the supplied threshold, but an iron-deficiency-without-anemia pattern may still be returned.

evidence.use: Provides the age/sex fallback CBC intervals used by the implementation from 6 months to under 18 years.
Supports explicit cutoff provenance and contextual interpretation of hemoglobin.

steps.caveat: A normal classification is only relative to the effective threshold. It does not rule out iron deficiency, evolving disease, mixed disorders, or an inappropriate reference interval.

steps.title: Classify red-cell morphology
steps.clinicalQuestion: Is the average red-cell volume below, within, or above the applicable interval, and is cell-size variability increased?
steps.summary: Classify MCV as microcytic, normocytic, or macrocytic, then use RDW and transfusion context to refine—but not prove—the differential.

inputs.purpose: Routes the case to microcytic, normocytic, or macrocytic pathways.
Preferred morphology thresholds.
Assesses red-cell size dispersion when an upper limit is available.
Preferred RDW threshold.
Adds a mixed-population caution because indices and smear may be altered.

equations.label: Morphology classifier
equations.expression: MCV < lower → microcytic; MCV > upper → macrocytic; otherwise → normocytic
equations.interpretation: The comparison uses local limits first and the AAP fallback second.

equations.label: RDW flag
equations.expression: rdwHigh = (RDW > RDWUpper)
equations.interpretation: RDW modifies pattern support; it does not independently establish an etiology.

equations.label: Mixed-population warning
equations.expression: recentTransfusion = true → add interpretation limitation
equations.interpretation: Transfused and native red-cell populations can mask or broaden MCV/RDW patterns.

outputs: Microcytic, normocytic, macrocytic, or indeterminate morphology
RDW high, not high, or unavailable
A morphology-specific branch selector
Transfusion-related or mixed-pattern cautions

examples.title: Microcytic with increased RDW
examples.data: 20-month-old female; MCV 64 fL; lower limit 73.3 fL; RDW 19.1%; upper limit 15.4%.
examples.result: Microcytic branch with increased size variability; iron studies become a high-value discriminator.

examples.title: Normocytic does not mean a narrow differential
examples.result: Normocytic branch; reticulocyte response separates loss/destruction from impaired production.

examples.title: Macrocytic routing
examples.data: 8-year-old female; MCV 96 fL; upper limit 87.7 fL.
examples.result: Macrocytic branch; reticulocytosis, B12/folate, thyroid, liver, medications, copper, and marrow signals are reviewed.

evidence.use: Provides fallback MCV and RDW intervals and supports CBC morphology in iron-deficiency evaluation.
Supports morphology-first organization of the broad pediatric differential and smear interpretation.

steps.caveat: MCV is an average. Mixed deficiencies, reticulocytosis, recent transfusion, or two opposing disorders can produce a deceptively normal value.

steps.title: Classify marrow response
steps.clinicalQuestion: Is erythropoiesis appropriately increased for the degree and timing of anemia?
steps.summary: Use the laboratory- and age-context interpretation of reticulocyte response to separate loss/destruction from inadequate production.

inputs.unit: low / inappropriately normal / appropriate / high / unknown
inputs.purpose: The actual branch input used by the engine.
Captured for context but not subjected to a universal hard-coded cutoff.
Needed clinically to judge whether a raw reticulocyte percentage is appropriate.
Can distort reticulocyte interpretation and specialized red-cell testing.
Raises transient or aplastic suppression pathways when response is low.

equations.label: Engine branch mapping
equations.expression: high → loss/destruction; low or inappropriately-normal → impaired production; appropriate → context-dependent; unknown → ask for interpretation
equations.interpretation: The engine deliberately consumes a clinician/laboratory interpretation rather than imposing one pediatric numeric cutoff.

equations.label: Reference calculation—not executed
equations.expression: corrected retic % = retic % × (patient Hct ÷ age-appropriate reference Hct)
equations.interpretation: Useful conceptually when a raw percentage is misleading in anemia; this prototype does not calculate it because hematocrit norms and assay context vary.

equations.label: Reference calculation—not executed
equations.expression: RPI = corrected retic % ÷ maturation correction
equations.interpretation: Shown for transparency only. No universal RPI threshold is encoded in the rules engine.

outputs: High-response loss/destruction branch
Low/inappropriately-normal production-limited branch
Context-dependent appropriate-response interpretation
Adaptive request when reticulocyte interpretation is missing

examples.title: High response plus hemolysis evidence
examples.data: Hemoglobin 8.7 g/dL; response high; indirect bilirubin high; LDH high; haptoglobin low; jaundice.
examples.evaluation: High response routes toward loss/destruction; multiple biochemical and clinical markers support red-cell destruction.
examples.result: Hemolytic-anemia pattern is ranked; DAT, smear, membrane/enzyme/hemoglobin studies refine cause.

examples.title: Low response plus multiple cytopenias
examples.data: Anemia; low response; leukopenia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia; blasts.
examples.evaluation: Low production signal plus abnormal additional cell lines and blasts.
examples.result: Urgent marrow-failure/infiltration/malignancy pathway rather than isolated nutritional anemia.

evidence.use: Supports reticulocyte response as the principal production-versus-loss/destruction discriminator in pediatric anemia.

steps.caveat: The response can lag after acute hemorrhage or hemolysis and can be altered by transfusion, severe nutrient deficiency, infection, renal disease, or marrow suppression.

steps.title: Apply morphology-specific rules
steps.clinicalQuestion: Which evidence-linked pattern best explains the combination of morphology, marrow response, iron/inflammation studies, smear, exposures, and history?
steps.summary: Dispatch to the morphology branch, then evaluate explicit compound conditions for iron deficiency, inflammation, globin disorders, lead, bleeding, hemolysis, renal/endocrine disease, nutrients, infection, and marrow disorders.

inputs.purpose: Distinguishes absolute iron deficiency from ferritin confounding and inflammatory restriction.
Adds directionality when ferritin alone is insufficient.
Supports iron deficiency or inflammation in the AAP framework when available.
Relatively high RBC count can support a globin-production pattern in persistent microcytosis.
Builds red-cell destruction and immune/nonimmune pathways.
Adds target cells, spherocytes, schistocytes, hypersegmentation, stippling, and blasts.
Adds bleeding, diet, inflammation, renal disease, medications, travel, inheritance, congenital phenotype, and organomegaly.

inputs.field: labs.b12 / folate / copper / TSH / liver / creatinine
inputs.purpose: Routes nutritional, endocrine, hepatic, renal, and marrow-production patterns.

equations.label: AAP ferritin threshold
equations.expression: ferritinThreshold = 30 if menstruating or age ≥ 144 months; otherwise 20 for age 6–143 months
equations.interpretation: Iron deficiency is supported when ferritin is at or below the applicable threshold; inflammation still requires contextual interpretation.

equations.label: sTfR/ferritin index
equations.expression: index = sTfR ÷ log10(ferritin)
equations.interpretation: The engine accepts the precomputed value: >2 supports iron deficiency; <1 with elevated CRP supports inflammation in the encoded AAP pathway. Assay compatibility matters.

equations.label: Inflammatory iron-restriction pattern
equations.expression: anemia ∧ CRP elevated ∧ ferritin not low ∧ TSAT low ∧ TIBC low-or-normal
equations.interpretation: A compound pattern—not any single datum—raises anemia of inflammation/inflammatory iron restriction.

equations.label: Biochemical hemolysis heuristic
equations.expression: hemolysisPattern = count(indirect bilirubin high, LDH high, haptoglobin low) ≥ 2
equations.interpretation: This transparent implementation heuristic triggers a pattern review; it is not presented as a validated diagnostic score.

outputs: One or more matched diagnostic patterns
Supporting findings and conflicting cautions
Pattern-specific confirmatory tests
Adaptive questions for missing high-value discriminators
Rule IDs and evidence IDs for every matched output

examples.title: Iron deficiency anemia pattern
examples.data: Microcytosis; RDW high; ferritin 6 ng/mL; threshold 20; CRP normal; low TSAT; dietary risk.
examples.evaluation: Anemia is present and ferritin is below the applicable threshold; morphology and history add support.
examples.result: Iron deficiency anemia reaches the highest defined pattern level; lead remains a possible parallel pathway when pica is present.

examples.title: Inflammatory iron restriction
examples.data: Normocytic anemia; CRP elevated; ferritin 85; TSAT low; TIBC low; sTfR/ferritin index 0.8.
examples.evaluation: Compound inflammatory pattern is present; index <1 adds support.
examples.result: Anemia of inflammation / inflammatory iron restriction becomes the dominant pattern while coexisting absolute iron deficiency is still considered clinically.

examples.title: Beta-thalassemia pattern
examples.data: Marked microcytosis; RDW not high; RBC relatively high; ferritin not low; target cells; HbA2 elevated.
examples.evaluation: Several globin-production clues and direct hemoglobin-analysis evidence match.
examples.result: Beta-thalassemia pattern is strongly supported; molecular or specialist confirmation depends on context.

evidence.use: Supplies the ferritin thresholds, inflammation caveats, iron-profile logic, sTfR/ferritin interpretation, and microcytic differentiation framework.
Supplies the broad morphology/reticulocyte differential across hemolytic, marrow, renal, infectious, nutritional, endocrine, and inherited causes.
Supplies the lead threshold and specimen-specific clinical pathway.
Supports quantitative G6PD testing and the caution that testing during acute hemolysis or soon after transfusion may be falsely normal.

steps.caveat: Pattern matches are intentionally nonexclusive. More than one disorder can coexist, especially iron deficiency plus inflammation, bleeding, lead exposure, hemoglobinopathy, or recent transfusion.

steps.title: Rank and expose provenance
steps.clinicalQuestion: How are matched rules merged into an inspectable work-list without presenting an unsupported probability?
steps.summary: Merge all matching rules by candidate, retain the strongest ordinal evidence label, sum deterministic priority points, and expose every input-derived finding, rule, caveat, next step, and citation.

inputs.purpose: Contribute candidate level, points, findings, cautions, next steps, and evidence IDs.
Provides stable labels, summaries, default next steps, and base evidence.
Sorted independently so urgent safety findings are not buried by differential ranking.
Sorted by explicit information-gathering priority.
Makes the inference reproducible and reviewable.

equations.label: Candidate level merge
equations.expression: candidate.level = max(levelRank of all matched rules)
equations.interpretation: Level order: meets-defined-pattern > strongly-supported > supported > possible > not-excluded.

equations.label: Priority score
equations.expression: candidate.score = Σ points from matched candidate rules
equations.interpretation: Points only break ties within the ordinal workflow; they are not probabilities or validated risk weights.

equations.label: Deterministic ordering
equations.expression: sort by levelRank ↓, then score ↓, then label A→Z
equations.interpretation: The same normalized input and knowledge-base version produce the same ordering.

equations.label: Alert ordering
equations.expression: emergency > urgent > important > informational
equations.interpretation: Safety outputs remain separate from etiologic candidate ranking.

outputs: Ranked diagnostic-pattern work-list
Separate safety alerts and interpretive notes
Matched rules, supporting findings, cautions, and next steps
Evidence links and knowledge-base version
Full downloadable audit JSON

examples.title: Why beta-thalassemia ranks first
examples.data: Rules for persistent microcytosis, globin-production clues, and elevated HbA2 all match.
examples.evaluation: The beta-thalassemia candidate receives the strongest matched level and its rule points are summed.
examples.result: It ranks above broader thalassemia-trait patterns, while both remain visible with their distinct evidence and next steps.

examples.title: Why a marrow alert is not just another candidate
examples.data: Blasts, fever with neutropenia, and multiple cytopenias.
examples.evaluation: Emergency/urgent alert rules and marrow candidate rules match in parallel.
examples.result: Alerts appear first; the marrow pattern remains in the differential with its matched rule provenance.

evidence.use: Supports making the basis of clinician-facing recommendations independently reviewable, including inputs, logic, and source rationale.
Provides the clinical framework represented by the candidate organization; it does not validate the app's internal point values.

steps.caveat: Neither candidate level nor point score is a likelihood ratio, posterior probability, diagnostic confidence, sensitivity, specificity, or treatment recommendation.

branches.title: Microcytic branch
branches.subtitle: Iron availability, inflammation, globin production, lead, blood loss, and rare iron-handling disorders
sequence: Confirm ferritin relative to the age/menstrual threshold.
Interpret non-low ferritin with CRP and the full iron profile.
Use sTfR/log10(ferritin) when available and assay-compatible.
Review RBC count pattern, RDW, smear, newborn screen, HbA2, and globin testing.
Evaluate lead level, specimen type, pica, and exposure history.
Escalate persistent iron-loading or treatment-refractory patterns for specialist review.

discriminators.supports: Ferritin ≤ threshold; often high RDW; low TSAT; compatible diet, bleeding, or malabsorption
discriminators.counterpoints: Inflammation can raise ferritin; a response-to-treatment history must be interpreted carefully

discriminators.supports: CRP elevated, ferritin non-low, TSAT low, TIBC low/normal; sTfR/ferritin index <1 can add support
discriminators.counterpoints: Absolute iron deficiency can coexist; low ferritin remains important

discriminators.supports: Persistent marked microcytosis, non-low ferritin, relatively high RBC count, target cells, family/newborn-screen clues, HbA2 or molecular findings
discriminators.counterpoints: CBC indices alone are screening clues, not definitive diagnosis

discriminators.supports: BLL ≥3.5 µg/dL, exposure risk, pica, stippling
discriminators.counterpoints: Capillary elevation needs venous confirmation; stippling is not specific

discriminators.supports: Microcytosis with high iron/ferritin by the reporting lab, stippling, or persistent refractory iron-restricted pattern
discriminators.counterpoints: Requires exclusion of common causes and specialist confirmation

branches.title: Normocytic branch
branches.subtitle: Reticulocyte response separates loss/destruction from impaired production
sequence: High response: evaluate bleeding and red-cell destruction.
Review bilirubin, LDH, haptoglobin, DAT, urine, and smear.
Low/inappropriate response: evaluate renal, inflammatory, viral, endocrine, and marrow causes.
Treat additional cytopenias, blasts, organomegaly, lymphadenopathy, petechiae, or bruising as red flags.
In chronic hemolytic disease, abrupt reticulocytopenia raises aplastic-crisis pathways.

discriminators.supports: Bleeding source/history with a high or appropriate response; iron deficiency may emerge with chronic loss
discriminators.counterpoints: Very acute loss can precede the reticulocyte rise

discriminators.supports: High response plus bilirubin/LDH/haptoglobin pattern, jaundice/dark urine, DAT, and smear clues
discriminators.counterpoints: Severe marrow suppression or nutrient deficiency can blunt the expected response

discriminators.supports: Low/inappropriate response plus renal dysfunction or chronic inflammation
discriminators.counterpoints: Iron deficiency and blood loss may coexist

discriminators.supports: Low response, recent viral illness, isolated anemia or chronic hemolytic disease with abrupt decline
discriminators.counterpoints: Requires exclusion of leukemia, congenital failure, renal disease, and other marrow pathology

discriminators.supports: Low response plus additional cytopenias, blasts, organomegaly, lymphadenopathy, bruising, or congenital phenotype
discriminators.counterpoints: Urgency is driven by the red flags, not by candidate score

branches.title: Macrocytic branch
branches.subtitle: Reticulocytosis, megaloblastic deficiency, endocrine/hepatic effects, medications, copper, and marrow disease
sequence: Determine whether high reticulocytes explain the elevated MCV.
With low/inappropriate response, review B12, folate, smear, and neurologic/nutritional history.
Review TSH, liver studies, and macrocytosis-associated medications.
Assess copper deficiency when nutrition, malabsorption, or cytopenia pattern supports it.
Escalate congenital phenotype, progressive cytopenias, or blasts to marrow-failure evaluation.

discriminators.supports: Macrocytosis with high reticulocyte response and loss/hemolysis evidence
discriminators.counterpoints: Does not exclude a concurrent nutrient disorder

discriminators.supports: Low/inappropriate response, low nutrient result, hypersegmented neutrophils, compatible diet/malabsorption
discriminators.counterpoints: Neurologic features and confirmatory biochemical testing matter; mixed iron deficiency can normalize MCV

discriminators.supports: Compatible local lab abnormality or medication exposure
discriminators.counterpoints: Association is not sufficient without clinical confirmation

discriminators.supports: Low copper with nutrition/malabsorption context and compatible cytopenias
Congenital phenotype, early onset, progressive cytopenias, low response, or blasts
discriminators.counterpoints: Urgent specialist evaluation precedes routine ranking

exampleCases.label: Iron deficiency anemia
exampleCases.description: 20-month-old with microcytosis, high RDW, ferritin 6, dietary risk, and pica.

exampleCases.label: Beta-thalassemia pattern
exampleCases.description: 10-year-old with marked microcytosis, high RBC count, target cells, and elevated HbA2.

exampleCases.label: Anemia of inflammation
exampleCases.description: 14-year-old with inflammatory bowel disease, elevated CRP, restricted iron profile, and low index.

exampleCases.label: Hemolysis / hereditary spherocytosis
exampleCases.description: 8-year-old with high reticulocyte response, jaundice, biochemical hemolysis, DAT negative, and spherocytes.

exampleCases.label: Elevated capillary lead
exampleCases.description: 3-year-old with microcytosis, pica, stippling, and capillary BLL 8.2 µg/dL.

exampleCases.label: Marrow red flags
exampleCases.description: 7-year-old with anemia, pancytopenia, fever, low reticulocyte response, and blasts.
